prompt: I have a DSPy ChainOfThought pipeline for question answering with about 100 training examples. I want to get the best possible accuracy. How should I optimize it?
expected_output: Uses MIPROv2 with auto preset, evaluates before and after, saves the result
assertions: Uses dspy.MIPROv2 as the optimizer (not BootstrapFewShot alone)
Passes metric as a required parameter to MIPROv2 constructor
Uses auto parameter (light, medium, or heavy) or sets num_candidates and num_trials manually
Passes trainset as a keyword argument to compile(): optimizer.compile(program, trainset=trainset)
Shows evaluation with Evaluate to measure improvement

prompt: I want to optimize my DSPy program but I am worried about cost. What are the different auto settings in MIPROv2 and how do I control the budget?
expected_output: Explains auto=light/medium/heavy with cost tradeoffs and manual override options
assertions: Describes all three auto levels: light, medium, heavy
Notes that light is the default auto setting
Mentions num_candidates and num_trials as manual alternatives to auto
Recommends starting with medium and upgrading to heavy only with large datasets
Mentions that auto=None requires both num_candidates and num_trials

prompt: I already ran BootstrapFewShot on my DSPy pipeline and got some improvement. Now I want to squeeze out more accuracy. Should I try MIPROv2 on top of it?
expected_output: Shows stacking pattern: run MIPROv2 on the bootstrapped result
assertions: Shows passing the bootstrapped program as the student to MIPROv2 compile()
Explains that MIPROv2 jointly optimizes instructions AND demos (unlike BootstrapFewShot which only does demos)
Includes evaluation before and after to measure the additional improvement
Uses trainset with .with_inputs() on each example
